flashcards.front: What is Slotted ALOHA and how does it improve upon pure ALOHA?
flashcards.back: Slotted ALOHA divides time into discrete slots equal to the packet transmission time. Nodes can only transmit at the beginning of each slot, reducing the vulnerable period from 2T to T, effectively doubling the maximum throughput to 37%.

flashcards.front: Explain the difference between 1-persistent, p-persistent, and non-persistent CSMA.
flashcards.back: In 1-persistent CSMA, node transmits immediately when channel is idle. In p-persistent CSMA (for slotted channels), node transmits with probability p when idle. Non-persistent CSMA waits a random time before checking again, reducing collisions but increasing delay.

flashcards.front: What is Binary Exponential Backoff algorithm used in Ethernet?
flashcards.back: After a collision, nodes wait a random number of slot times (0 to 2^n-1) before retrying, where n is the number of collisions (capped at 10). The backoff range doubles after each collision, reducing probability of further collisions.

flashcards.front: How does Token Ring protocol work?
flashcards.back: A special packet called a token circulates on the ring. A node can transmit only when it possesses the token. After transmission, it releases the token. This eliminates collisions entirely, ensuring deterministic access.

flashcards.front: What is Token Bus and how does it differ from Token Ring?
flashcards.back: Token Bus (IEEE 802.4) uses a logical ring formed on a physical bus/cable. Stations pass a token logically, not physically around a ring. It combines token-passing reliability with broadcast bus topology.

Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection: nodes listen before transmitting. If collision is detected during transmission, all nodes stop and send jam signal. This ensures frames are corrupted for only ~2τ propagation time.

flashcards.front: Compare TDMA, FDMA, and CDMA multiple access techniques.
flashcards.back: TDMA (Time Division) assigns unique time slots to users. FDMA (Frequency Division) assigns unique frequency bands. CDMA (Code Division) allows all users to transmit simultaneously using unique codes, providing soft capacity.

flashcards.front: What is MACA (Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) and how does it work?
flashcards.back: MACA uses RTS/CTS (Request to Send/Clear to Send) handshake before data transmission. Sender sends RTS, receiver replies with CTS, then data is sent. This avoids hidden terminal problems by notifying neighboring nodes of impending transmission.
